resources.string: Number of points on the map
The phone is connected to this tower
The local OpenCellID cache is empty. Cell tower download has been queued.
Enter numeric Cell ID or Area
Tower added to the local SantiWay blacklist. Android does not allow a normal app to force the modem to disconnect from a tower or select another base station.
Tower added to the blacklist. Root access was granted, and radio reconnect was requested. The modem and carrier network choose the next tower.
Tower added to the blacklist, but the root reconnect command failed on this device.
An unknown Bluetooth address marked as ALARM was detected in the area. Verification is required.
A folder with this name already exists.
API key is not configured in the app
Device name saved and sent to the server!
Settings saved. Device name was not changed.
A folder with this name already exists!
Normal scanning cannot be started during a snapshot
The snapshot has no origin point. Location is required when creating it.
Trigger is armed, waiting for current location
Trigger armed. Move 500 m away from the snapshot point. Current distance: %1$.0f m
All requested permissions are required for the app to work correctly. Without them, scanning and location detection will not be possible.
Enable the following features for the app to work correctly:\n\n
• Mobile internet or Wi-Fi (for sending data)\n
\nWithout these features, data may be collected incompletely or incorrectly.

To scan Wi-Fi and Bluetooth devices, the app needs:

• Location access (for device detection)
• Bluetooth scan permission (Android 12+)

These permissions are required for the main app functionality.
Allow notifications to receive scanning information?
Current scan folder is not defined.
Are you sure you want to delete the folder «%1$s»? All data will be lost!
File: %1$s (%2$d bytes). The type is not recognized for direct display. If this text is visible, the MIME type does not match "image/*" or "application/vnd.android.package-archive".
This is a system cell folder. Remove devices from it with the row button.
Error: signal strength must be between -120 and 0 dBm
The main folder cannot be renamed
Folders are shaking: drag them left or right, or tap the cross to delete
The main folder cannot be deleted
Automatically connecting to saved ESP32 devices
Disconnect this ESP32 and delete it with all saved data?
Connect the ESP32 using USB OTG. Use one merged firmware.bin. Do not unplug it while flashing.
Waiting for an ESP32 USB connection…
Could not open the USB port
No firmware.bin is bundled. Select a merged file manually.
Could not read the firmware file
ESP32 on map: %1$d · last update: %2$s ago
No triangulated devices at this point
Configure connection, map, language, and scanning behavior here.
Server values come from the app configuration. The device name is sent to the server.

A detailed guide to SantiWay: phone setup, scanning, device database, maps, ESP32 mesh, triangulation, ALARM, OpenCellID, notifications, server sync, USB flashing, background services, local databases, protocols, and data maintenance.
Grant the requested permissions, check the server and API in settings, choose language and geolocation protocol, then return to the main screen and start scanning.
Scanning collects Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular observations with the selected coordinate source. Snapshot mode saves a short control measurement without mixing it into a regular session.
Open the database to review discovered devices. Search by name or MAC, mark devices as TARGET, SAFE, or GREY, rename folders, and clear records when a session is no longer needed.
The map shows saved detections, selected-device history, the current point, and the route of coordinate changes. Large sessions use a display point limit.
ESP32 devices act as remote scanners and Wi-Fi mesh nodes. They talk to each other over Wi-Fi/ESP-NOW, while the phone connects and reconnects to saved ESP32 devices over Bluetooth LE.
The ESP32 map shows mesh nodes, triangulated discovered devices, 5-meter point clusters, and a fresh-first triangulation table when a cluster is tapped.
The app bundles firmware.bin. Connect ESP32 to the phone with USB OTG and flash it from the ESP32 section without a computer.
ALARM controls which alerts are allowed: off, tracking devices only, unknown cell towers only, or everything. The bell color shows the active mode.
The notifications screen stores alert and system history. TARGET alerts, unknown OpenCellID towers, and information events use different types and colors.
The app keeps a local OpenCellID cache of known towers for the Russian Federation, refreshes it once per day, and explains why a detected tower is considered illegitimate.

Background sync uploads discovered devices, phone coordinates, names, and folders to the configured server only when data upload is enabled in settings. A code path also exists for WebSocket notifications and APK updates.
The app uses several foreground services: Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, cellular scanning, ESP32 connections, and server upload. Their behavior depends on Android permissions and battery optimization settings.

Data is split across local SQLite databases: the main scanner database, ESP32 mesh, notification history, and OpenCellID cache. Cleanup removes old history while preserving important statuses and ESP32 positions.
The app has fixed time windows, batch limits, ESP32 record formats, RSSI-distance bounds, USB flashing parameters, and map-cache behavior. Use this section to check the real operating boundaries.
Settings control language, server upload, API, coordinate protocol, snapshot duration, static point, map layer, marker size, point limit, phone name, ALARM, and data retention.
If data does not appear, check Android permissions, Bluetooth, USB OTG, server, OpenCellID, three ESP32 devices with coordinates, and the 15-second measurement window.

1. Grant location, Bluetooth, notifications, and foreground-service permissions. Android blocks Wi-Fi/Bluetooth scanning without them.

2. Open settings and check phone name, language, server, API, coordinate protocol, static point if needed, map layer, marker size, and data retention.

3. Choose the ALARM mode: off, tracking devices only, unknown cell towers only, or all alerts.

4. If ESP32 mesh is used, connect ESP32 devices first, assign coordinates to at least three stationary nodes, and make sure they hear each other.

5. Start the needed mode: regular scanning for a long session or snapshot for a short control measurement.

6. After collecting data, use the device database, regular map, ESP32 map, notifications, and triangulation table to verify the result.

Regular scanning runs in cycles: the app collects Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular observations, adds the current coordinate source, and saves each record to the selected folder.

The Wi-Fi scanner stores SSID, BSSID, RSSI, frequency, capabilities/vendor, and coordinates. The Bluetooth scanner combines BLE and classic discovery and stores name, MAC, RSSI, and vendor. The cell scanner stores GSM/UMTS/LTE/5G parameters: cell id, LAC/TAC, MCC, MNC, PSC/PCI, frequencies, signal, and registration type.

Snapshot mode is for a short control point. It creates a snapshot-yyyy-MM-dd-HH-mm folder, scans for about 60 seconds, then restores the previous folder and scan state.

The coordinate source can be live GPS, a static point, or ESP32 mesh. If ESP32 mesh provides a fresh phone position, new records use that position. If the mesh position becomes stale, the app falls back to the regular source.

ESP32 records arrive over Bluetooth from the nearest connected ESP32 and over Wi-Fi mesh from distant ESP32 nodes. The app stores source ESP32, transport type, MAC/BSSID, RSSI, details, hops, and detection time.

The device database shows Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular records found by the phone scanner and ESP32 nodes.

TARGET marks a device that should be watched carefully. SAFE means known or allowed. GREY leaves it neutral.

Search works by name, MAC/BSSID, and saved fields. Filters show all records, TARGET only, or SAFE only. Bulk actions can move visible devices to SAFE, TARGET, or GREY.

Folders separate different surveys so places and days do not mix. They can be created, renamed, deleted, and reordered.

Device history opens a map with the latest coordinates and movement path. JSON export includes name, MAC, type, status, and coordinate history. If a record came from ESP32 triangulation, it is mirrored into the main database and can be uploaded to the server through the normal upload flow.

The regular map shows the selected device history. The newest point uses the main marker, and intermediate points are connected by a line so movement over time is visible.

Every map has a right drawer with address search, layer selection, and actions for the current map. Layers include OSM, Google, and Yandex, with satellite and hybrid variants. The layer selected in settings opens by default.

Address search works like a normal map search: for example, "Moscow, Mozhayskogo Street 32". After the search, the map centers on the found point and adds a marker.

Downloaded tiles are cached. System zoom buttons are hidden and replaced by unified + and - buttons. Marker size is configured in settings and applied across maps.

The map point limit affects display only, not storage. It keeps large sessions from slowing the map.

If static coordinates are enabled, new records use the selected point instead of live GPS. This is useful for stationary measurements, tests, and places where GPS is unstable.

The ESP32 map is different: it shows ESP32 nodes, the phone mesh estimate, and separate triangulation results for discovered devices.

Each ESP32 scans nearby Wi-Fi and Bluetooth. ESP32 devices relay data over Wi-Fi/ESP-NOW without a router, while the phone connects and reconnects to saved ESP32 devices over Bluetooth LE.

If the phone is far from some nodes, distant ESP32 devices relay records through neighboring ESP32 devices. The record keeps the original ESP32 and mesh hop count.

Coordinates require anchor ESP32 devices. An anchor is an ESP32 with manual coordinates or fresh auto-coordinates. A non-anchor ESP32 updates its own position only when at least three current anchors heard it inside a coherent time window.

The phone position is calculated the same way: the phone emits a BLE beacon, ESP32 devices hear its RSSI, and the app selects a fresh measurement group and estimates the coordinate from all suitable anchors.

ESP32 does not store unbounded history: firmware uses fixed buffers, clears Wi-Fi/BLE scan results, and removes old mesh packets. Long-term history is stored and cleaned on the phone.

The ESP32 Map button opens a separate screen. Green square markers are ESP32 devices with coordinates. Orange round markers are discovered devices with successful triangulation. The blue marker is the phone when its position was estimated through mesh.

The Triangulate discovered devices button takes recent Wi-Fi/Bluetooth observations and calculates coordinates only for devices heard by at least three ESP32 devices.

The algorithm uses all suitable anchor ESP32 devices, not only three. Measurements for one device must also be close in time: the maximum difference between ESP32 observations is 15 seconds. This prevents mixing a fresh observation with an old one after the device has moved away.

If 4 or more anchors are available and height differs enough, the app tries to estimate altitude. If altitude is unreliable, it uses stable 2D estimation and weighted altitude from ESP32 nodes.

Devices without a triangulation result are not shown on the map. Points within 5 meters are merged into one cluster. Tapping a cluster opens the triangulation table sorted from newest detections to oldest.

The ESP32 firmware screen lets you update ESP32 from the phone. Connect ESP32 through USB OTG, wait for the USB port, grant Android USB access, and start flashing.

The app uses bundled merged firmware.bin from assets/esp32/firmware.bin. It is a complete image for offset 0x0, so the user does not need separate bootloader, partitions, and app files.

Do not unplug USB while writing. If flashing does not start, check the OTG adapter, data cable, ESP32 power, USB permission, bootloader mode, and selected firmware.bin.

After successful flashing, ESP32 advertises the SantiWay BLE service, participates in Wi-Fi mesh, cleans temporary buffers, and sends records to the phone directly or through nearby ESP32 nodes.

ALARM has three independent channels: illegitimate cell towers are yellow, movement-based tracking detection is blue, and already marked TARGET devices are red. Channels can be combined, giving 8 states: off, three single channels, three pairs, and all channels together. The bell color is mixed from the enabled channels: blue+yellow becomes green, red+yellow becomes orange, red+blue becomes purple, and all three become white.

The tracking algorithm analyzes device history. The first sighting remains GREY. Small jumps under 10 meters are treated as noise. Large movements and accumulated distance over 24 hours can mark a device as TARGET and create an alert.

The red channel reacts to devices the user has already marked as TARGET. If only the red channel is enabled, the app does not run tracking detection and does not alert for cell towers.

Quiet mode converts every new device that is not green SAFE into TARGET. When scanning starts, the app asks whether cellular towers should also be treated as TARGET devices for this session. The main-screen bell quickly toggles off/all channels.

Notification history is stored separately from the main device database. It contains ALARM, SYSTEM, INFO, and CELL_UNKNOWN events.

Red notifications are tracking-device alerts. Orange notifications are unknown OpenCellID tower alerts. Green system messages are not removed by normal list cleanup; other notifications can be deleted.

Tapping a notification opens a detail screen. If coordinates are present, it can be opened on the map. If the notification is related to an APK update, the detail screen shows the install action.

Repeated alerts for the same device are rate-limited so one event does not flood the user.

OpenCellID is used as a local reference of known cell towers. The app gets the OpenCellID file list, downloads only the Russian Federation package, extracts the gzip CSV, imports it into a temporary table, and replaces the old table only after a successful import. If new data is not downloaded or import does not finish, the old database is kept.

Sync is scheduled once per day with WorkManager. If there is no successful baseline yet or more than 24 hours have passed, the app queues an extra sync.

When a tower is detected, the app normalizes the radio type: 5G becomes NR, WCDMA becomes UMTS, LTE/NR use TAC, and GSM/UMTS use LAC. The lookup key is radio/MCC/MNC/area/cell.

A tower is considered illegitimate for two reasons. First: that key is missing from the local OpenCellID database. Second: the tower exists in the database, but the current detection point is too far from its legitimate location; the threshold is the greater of 10 km and three OpenCellID range radii.

The notification explains the reason: missing from the database or too far from the legitimate point. For a far tower, the app stores both the legitimate coordinates and the current detection coordinates; the map draws a line between them.

DeviceUploadService runs in the background only when Send data to server is enabled in settings. When the switch is off, the upload service is not started, the worker skips sending, and the managers for coordinates, devices, names, and folders do not make network requests.

When upload is enabled, the service checks the network about once per minute, uploads phone location, and sends a batch of new detections. Records are uploaded by timestamp in batches up to 100 and receive the is_uploaded flag after success.

The server payload includes phone device_id, coordinates, RSSI, network type, device name, folder, system folder, API key, and phone identifier. Device names and folders are synchronized by separate API requests.

If the network is unavailable or the server does not respond, records stay in the local database and are retried later after upload is enabled. Upload uses the configured server domain and API key.

The code also contains a WebSocket/APK channel: it can receive notifications, APK chunks, assemble an APK, and start installation through FileProvider. In the current build, automatic WebSocket start from MainActivity is disabled, so the main active update paths are ESP32 USB flashing and normal server upload.

Language changes the interface localization. Coordinate protocol decides what location is saved for new scans: GPS, static point, or a fresh ESP32 mesh estimate.

Static point is useful for tests, indoor work, and stationary measurements. Choose the point on a map or enter latitude and longitude manually.

Server and API key are used to upload detections, phone coordinates, device names, and folders. The phone name is sent with the data to identify the source.

Default map layer controls which tiles open on every map: OSM, Google, or Yandex. Yandex can require a Tiles API key. Marker size is set in dp and applies across maps.

Scanner settings control Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and Cell enablement, interval, and signal threshold. Map point limit affects history display only, not storage.

Data retention affects the database: old main records and temporary ESP32 tables are cleaned automatically. The default retention is 7 days, but the user can choose another value.

ESP32 positions are not removed by normal cleanup: node coordinates and their update time are preserved. Only old observations, mesh links, phone samples, connection history, and old triangulation results are deleted.

If ESP32 does not connect: check Bluetooth, Nearby devices/Location permissions, ESP32 power, and that the module is flashed with SantiWay firmware.

If mesh does not relay distant records: ESP32 devices must run the same mesh firmware, be within Wi-Fi/ESP-NOW hearing range of each other, and stay powered during scanning.

If ESP32 coordinates do not update: at least three ESP32 devices with current or static coordinates must hear that ESP32 within 15 seconds. Manual coordinates on stationary nodes count as anchors.

If a discovered device does not appear on the ESP32 map: it must be heard by at least three ESP32 devices with coordinates, the measurements must fit the 15-second window, and triangulation must produce a valid result.

If tower alerts do not appear: check that OpenCellID has downloaded a baseline, ALARM mode includes cell towers, and the detected tower is really absent from the local cache.

If data does not upload: check internet access, server domain, API key, and that DeviceUploadService is running. Pending records stay local and retry later.

If the triangulation table is empty after tapping a point: the cluster may have been cleaned by retention or there may be no records within 5 meters.

If USB flashing does not see ESP32: check OTG adapter, data cable, USB permission, board power, and switch ESP32 to bootloader mode if needed.
Tower was not found in the local OpenCellID cache: %1$s, signal %2$d dBm.
Orange alerts for cellular towers missing from OpenCellID.
Choose alert channels. Channels can be combined, and the bell color is mixed from the enabled modes.
Quiet mode: everything not green becomes TARGET
Should cellular towers be treated as TARGET devices for this scanning session?
ALARM mode is on for everything
Yandex requires a Tiles API key. OSM is opened temporarily.

Background work is split across services. MainActivity starts WifiForegroundService, BluetoothForegroundService, and CellForegroundService when scanning begins. Each service shows a foreground notification so Android does not stop collection immediately after the app is minimized.

Esp32ConnectionService runs separately: it discovers ESP32 devices by the SantiWay BLE service, connects to saved nodes, enables GATT characteristic notifications, advertises the phone BLE beacon, and receives records from ESP32 devices. Esp32BootReceiver restarts the ESP32 service after reboot when saved ESP32 devices already exist in the database.

DeviceUploadService checks the network about once per minute, uploads phone coordinates, and sends new records. OpenCellID uses WorkManager and can enqueue an extra sync if the local baseline has not been downloaded yet or is stale.

Full operation requires location, Wi-Fi/Bluetooth scanning access, Nearby devices on Android 12+, Bluetooth Scan/Connect/Advertise, notifications on Android 13+, and USB permission while flashing ESP32. If a permission is missing, the related service skips the unavailable part instead of crashing.

Android battery optimization can delay scanning, BLE advertising, and upload. For long sessions, allow background work for the app and disable aggressive battery optimization.

UnifiedScanner.db is the main app database. It stores the main table, user folders, *_unique tables for unique devices, target_devices and safe_devices status tables, coordinates, RSSI, network type, folder, is_uploaded flag, and upload fields.

esp32_devices.db stores ESP32 mesh data: connections, observations, esp_devices, mesh_links, phone_samples, and triangulated_devices. ESP32 positions and their update time are preserved as important state. Cleanup removes old observations, mesh links, phone samples, connection history, and old triangulation results.

notifications_history.db stores ALARM, SYSTEM, INFO, and CELL_UNKNOWN history, including text, time, coordinates, and device identifier. Notifications are separate from the main database so deleting a device record does not break the alert log.

opencellid_cache.db stores the local OpenCellID tower baseline. Import uses a temporary cell_towers_new table, then deletes the old cell_towers table and replaces it with the new one. Inserts are committed in batches of 5000 rows.

Retention is configured in settings and defaults to 7 days. ESP32 runtime cleanup has an effective minimum of 1 day so very small values do not remove fresh service data. Static and fresh ESP32 coordinates are not deleted by normal cleanup.

Scan intervals in code: Wi-Fi every 15 seconds, Bluetooth every 15 seconds with an active phase of about 8 seconds, and Cell every 10 seconds. Snapshot mode lasts the number of seconds configured in settings; the default is 60. The Bluetooth RSSI threshold defaults to -120 dBm and can be changed through scanner settings.

ESP32 BLE uses service UUID 7a1e0001-8e7f-4d8d-a7f4-2c6e6d520001 and data UUID 7a1e0002-8e7f-4d8d-a7f4-2c6e6d520001. The phone advertises a beacon with manufacturer id 0x02E5. ESP32 BLE discovery runs for about 8 seconds, repeats about every 12 seconds, connection timeout is about 12 seconds, and requested MTU is up to 247.

ESP32 records arrive as text split by | into at most 5 fields. Record types are W for Wi-Fi, B for Bluetooth, P for phone position, M for mesh link, and R for relay or a record from another node. The app stores source ESP32, device MAC/BSSID, RSSI, details, hops, and receive time.

ESP32 coordinates are fresh for 120 seconds. Observations for discovered-device triangulation are taken from the last 10 minutes, and measurements for one device across ESP32 nodes must fit inside a 15-second window. RSSI distance is clamped to 0.5-80 meters so one noisy signal cannot dominate the calculation. ESP32 map clusters merge points within 5 meters.

Server upload uses batches up to 100 records, up to 3 attempts per batch, and only runs when data upload is enabled. DeviceUploadService wakes about once per minute. OpenCellID downloads at most once every 24 hours; network connect timeout is 30 seconds and read timeout is 120 seconds. OpenCellID uses the Russian Federation package.

USB flashing validates that firmware.bin looks like an ESP32 image: magic 0xE9 must be at the start of the file or at offset 0x1000. The port opens as 115200 8N1 and writes 1024-byte blocks.

Actual radio range is not fixed by code and depends on board, antenna, power, walls, and interference. Practical guidance: phone BLE is often meters to tens of meters indoors, while Wi-Fi/ESP-NOW between ESP32 devices is usually tens of meters indoors and more in open space. A stable mesh needs neighboring ESP32 devices to hear each other reliably, not only appear occasionally during scans.
Settings contain unsaved changes. Save them before leaving?
Choose which additional devices in this folder should also become red.
Mark green SAFE devices as TARGET
Could not get current location for the trigger
Trigger for "%1$s" is armed. Move 500 m away from the current point.
The first period selects unique MAC addresses of grey devices in this folder. The second period searches those MACs in selected folders; if no folders are selected, all folders are used.
Tap a field to choose date and time. Format: 2026-06-23 18:30. You may fill only start or only end.
If no folders are selected, the search will run across all folders.
The period is enabled, but both start and end are empty
Period start cannot be after period end
Select a MAC from the list to build the histogram
Tap a bar to inspect its period.
This MAC has no points with coordinates

1. Grant location, Bluetooth, notifications, and foreground-service permissions. Android blocks Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular scanning without them.

2. Open settings and check phone name, language, server, API, coordinate protocol, static point if needed, map layer, marker size, point limit, and data retention.

3. Choose ALARM channels: tracking devices are blue, unknown cell towers are yellow, and manually marked TARGET devices are red. Channels can be combined; quiet mode turns new non-SAFE devices into TARGET.

4. If ESP32 mesh is used, connect ESP32 devices first, assign coordinates to at least three stationary nodes, and make sure they hear each other over Wi-Fi/ESP-NOW.

5. Use regular scanning for long sessions and snapshot for a control point. After a snapshot, the trigger button can turn devices from the snapshot folder into TARGET after you move 500 meters away; SAFE devices are not changed.

6. After collecting data, use the device database, regular map, ESP32 map, cell tower map layer, notifications, triangulation table, and grey-device search.

Regular scanning runs in cycles: the app collects Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular observations, adds the current coordinate source, and saves each record to the selected folder.

The Wi-Fi scanner stores SSID, BSSID, RSSI, frequency, capabilities/vendor, and coordinates. The Bluetooth scanner combines BLE and classic discovery and stores name, MAC, RSSI, and vendor. The cell scanner stores GSM/UMTS/LTE/5G parameters: cell id, LAC/TAC, MCC, MNC, PSC/PCI, frequencies, signal, and registration type.

Snapshot mode is for a short control point. It creates a snapshot-yyyy-MM-dd-HH-mm folder, scans for the duration configured in settings, 60 seconds by default, then restores the previous folder and scan state.

After a snapshot, the trigger button remembers the snapshot location and turns devices from that folder into TARGET only after the user moves 500 meters away. Devices marked SAFE stay green.

The coordinate source can be live GPS, static point, or ESP32 mesh. If ESP32 mesh provides a fresh phone coordinate, new records use it; if the mesh coordinate is stale, the app falls back to the normal source.

For long scans, services use non-blocking best-effort location, clear temporary deduplication sets between cycles, and do not keep unlimited history in memory. Old history is removed by the retention setting.

The device database shows Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and cellular records found by the phone scanner and ESP32 nodes.

TARGET marks a device that should be watched carefully. SAFE means known or allowed. GREY leaves it neutral.

The list has two independent toggles. The first chooses record type: All, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, or Cell towers. The second chooses status: TARGET, all, or SAFE. Selected values are saved and restored the next time the list opens.

System cell folders Alert, Safe, and Blacklist show the matching tower groups; records can be removed from them with the row button.

Search works by name, MAC/BSSID, and saved fields. Bulk actions can move visible devices to SAFE, TARGET, or GREY.

Folders separate different surveys. They can be created, renamed, deleted, and reordered. In move mode the folders tremble, can be dragged, and the cross opens a delete confirmation dialog.

When an entire folder is marked TARGET, the app asks whether cell towers should also become TARGET and whether green SAFE devices should become TARGET. By default, SAFE devices are not changed without explicit user choice.

A long press on a folder can start grey-device search. The first period selects unique grey MACs from that folder, and the second period searches those MACs in selected folders or all folders. Date fields open a calendar and time picker, then fill yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm. The result opens a map, a unique-MAC list with detection counts, and an interactive histogram; selecting a MAC clears the map and shows only points for that device.

Device history opens a map with the latest coordinates and movement path. If a record came from ESP32 triangulation, it is mirrored into the main database and can be uploaded through the normal upload flow.

The regular map shows the selected device history. The newest point uses the main marker, and intermediate points are connected by a line so movement over time is visible.

Every map has a full-height right drawer with address search, layer selection, and actions for the current map. When the drawer is open, map buttons and information panels are hidden except for the system back arrow.

Layers include OSM, Google, and Yandex, with satellite and hybrid variants. The layer selected in settings opens by default.

Address search works like a normal map search: for example, "Moscow, Mozhayskogo Street 32". After the search, the map centers on the found point and adds a marker.

The drawer includes a cell tower switch and numeric search by Cell ID or Area. The map does not load every tower at once: OpenCellID towers are queried only for the visible map bounds, and the tower currently serving the phone is highlighted separately.

The drawer also shows the connected tower. The change button adds it to the local blacklist. On a normal phone Android does not let the app force another base station; on a rooted phone the app additionally requests radio reconnect through su, but the modem and carrier network still choose the next tower.

Downloaded tiles are cached. System and old centered zoom buttons are hidden and replaced by round + and - buttons at the bottom right; one tap changes zoom 3 times stronger than before. Marker size is configured in settings and applied across maps.

The map point limit affects display only, not storage. It keeps large sessions from slowing the map.

The ESP32 map shows ESP32 nodes, the phone mesh estimate, and separate triangulation results for discovered devices. Devices without triangulation results are not shown there.

ALARM has three independent channels: tracking devices, unknown cell towers, and manually marked TARGET devices. They can be enabled separately or together, and the bell color shows the active combination.

ALARM has three independent channels: illegitimate cell towers are yellow, movement-based tracking detection is blue, and already marked TARGET devices are red. Channels can be combined, giving 8 states: off, three single channels, three pairs, and all channels together. The bell color is mixed from the enabled channels: blue+yellow becomes green, red+yellow becomes orange, red+blue becomes purple, and all three become white.

The tracking algorithm analyzes device history. The first sighting remains GREY. Small jumps under 10 meters are treated as noise. If the distance between the first and second point is greater than 10 km and speed is greater than 400 km/h, the device is marked as GPS spoofing and is no longer automatically moved to TARGET. Other large movements and accumulated distance over 24 hours can mark a device as TARGET and create an alert.

The red channel reacts only to devices the user has already marked as TARGET. If only the red channel is enabled, the app does not run tracking detection and does not alert for cell towers.

Quiet mode converts every new device that is not green SAFE into TARGET. When scanning starts, the app asks which variant to use: treat cell towers as TARGET devices or do not. The main-screen bell quickly toggles off/all channels, while the detailed channel mask is stored in settings.

Language changes the interface localization. Every new UI element should have strings in the base, English, Arabic, and Chinese locales; when a setting is added, update all `values-*` files and keep placeholders such as %1$d identical.

Coordinate protocol decides what location is saved for new scans: GPS, static point, or a fresh ESP32 mesh estimate. Static point is useful for tests, indoor work, and stationary measurements. Choose the point on a map or enter latitude and longitude manually.

The data-upload switch controls all server upload. When it is off, new network sends for detections, coordinates, names, and folders are not performed; local records remain in the database.

Server and API key are used to upload detections, phone coordinates, device names, and folders when upload is enabled. The phone name is sent with the data to identify the source.

Snapshot duration is set in seconds and defaults to 60. Snapshot mode uses this value when it starts and shows it in the notification/message.

Default map layer controls which tiles open on every map: OSM, Google, or Yandex. Yandex can require a Tiles API key. Marker size is set in dp and applies across maps.

Scanner settings control Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, and Cell enablement, interval, and signal threshold. Map point limit affects history display only, not storage.

ALARM is configured with separate switches: blue tracking channel, yellow unknown-tower channel, red marked-TARGET channel, all channels, and off. Quiet mode is stored separately and can include or exclude cell towers when scanning starts.

If the user changes settings and leaves without saving, the app asks whether to save changes or exit without saving.

Data retention affects the database: old main records and temporary ESP32 tables are cleaned automatically. The default retention is 7 days, but the user can choose another value. ESP32 positions are not removed by normal cleanup.

If ESP32 does not connect: check Bluetooth, Nearby devices/Location permissions, ESP32 power, and that the module is flashed with SantiWay firmware.

If mesh does not relay distant records: ESP32 devices must run the same mesh firmware, be within Wi-Fi/ESP-NOW hearing range of each other, and stay powered during scanning.

If ESP32 coordinates do not update: at least three ESP32 devices with current or static coordinates must hear that ESP32 within 15 seconds. Manual coordinates on stationary nodes count as anchors.

If a discovered device does not appear on the ESP32 map: it must be heard by at least three ESP32 devices with coordinates, the measurements must fit the 15-second window, and triangulation must produce a valid result.

If there are no cell tower alerts: confirm OpenCellID has downloaded the baseline, ALARM includes cell towers, and the found tower is truly missing from the local cache.

If the cell tower map layer is empty: zoom to the needed area, enable the tower layer in the right drawer, and wait for the visible bounds to load.

If changing tower does not drop the connection: on a non-rooted phone this is an Android limitation. On a rooted phone, check that the root manager granted SantiWay su access; even after a successful command, the modem chooses the exact next tower.

If the UI slows after long scanning: reduce the map point limit, check data retention, and restart scanning. Services clear temporary sets between cycles, but very large local databases still benefit from regular cleanup.

If USB flashing does not see ESP32: check OTG adapter, data cable, USB permission, board power, and bootloader mode.

Reason: the tower exists in OpenCellID, but was detected too far from its legitimate location. Data: %1$s. Distance: %2$s, allowed up to %3$s. Legitimate point: %4$.6f, %5$.6f. Current point: %6$.6f, %7$.6f.
